serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of unsalted butter is approximately 5 g (1 teaspoon), as used for spreading on bread or finishing a cooked dish.

nutrient_source.title: Butter, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 1001)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for unsalted butter (FDC ID 1001) used as the primary approved source for all nutrient values, cross-validated against HPB Singapore values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol. HPB Singapore values are consistent with USDA SR Legacy. USDA SR Legacy is the authoritative source for saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrients.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 1001)
gi_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
gi_source.source_match_notes: Unsalted butter contains only 0.7 g carbohydrate per 100 g as confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 1001). Pure fat foods do not elicit a meaningful glycemic response and are not assigned a GI value by the University of Sydney GI Database or any peer-reviewed GI research. GI is set to null and gi_category to 'not_applicable' per the Veritas critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by convention.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high saturated fat content (~51 g/100 g) contributing to arterial stiffness and cardiovascular risk; sodium is low at 10 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Despite its very low sodium content (10 mg/100 g), unsalted butter's extremely high saturated fat (~51 g/100 g) significantly raises LDL cholesterol and promotes arterial stiffness, making it a high-concern food for individuals with hypertension; limit to occasional use and substitute with extra-virgin olive oil or avocado-based spreads where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.7 g/100 g) causes no direct glycemic spike, but very high saturated fat (~51 g/100 g) may worsen insulin resistance with habitual consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Unsalted butter does not raise blood glucose directly due to its negligible carbohydrate content (0.7 g/100 g), but its very high saturated fat content (~51 g/100 g) may impair insulin sensitivity over time; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should use it sparingly and prefer unsaturated fat sources such as olive oil.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Extremely high saturated fat (~51 g/100 g), trans fat (~2.6 g/100 g), and cholesterol (252 mg/100 g) all elevate LDL and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Unsalted butter is a high-concern food for cardiovascular disease due to its combination of very high saturated fat (~51 g/100 g), naturally occurring trans fat (~2.6 g/100 g), and high cholesterol (252 mg/100 g), all of which raise LDL cholesterol; the AHA recommends limiting saturated fat to less than 6% of total daily calories and replacing butter with unsaturated fat sources such as olive oil or canola oil.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (~51 g/100 g) and trans fat (~2.6 g/100 g) are both pro-inflammatory and exceed RA hard-gate thresholds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Unsalted butter is strongly pro-inflammatory for rheumatoid arthritis due to its very high saturated fat (~51 g/100 g) and naturally occurring trans fat (~2.6 g/100 g), both of which promote systemic inflammation; replace with extra-virgin olive oil, which provides oleocanthal with documented anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Unsalted butter is a dairy fat product made by churning pasteurised cream derived from cow's milk. It contains no added salt, distinguishing it from salted butter. The product is composed predominantly of milk fat (~83 g/100 g), with very small amounts of protein (0.6 g/100 g) and carbohydrate (0.7 g/100 g). Its energy density is very high at approximately 742 kcal per 100 g. The fat profile is dominated by saturated fatty acids (~51 g/100 g), with moderate monounsaturated fat (~21 g/100 g), small amounts of polyunsaturated fat (~3 g/100 g), and naturally occurring trans fat (~2.6 g/100 g from ruminant sources). Cholesterol content is high at 252 mg/100 g. Unsalted butter has a rich, creamy flavour with a smooth, spreadable texture at room temperature and a firm texture when chilled. It is widely used in baking, cooking, and as a spread, and is a staple ingredient in Western, European, and global cuisines. Clinically, unsalted butter is a high-concern food for cardiovascular disease and hypertension due to its very high saturated fat and trans fat content, and a moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to potential effects on insulin resistance. Its negligible carbohydrate content means it has no direct glycemic impact. It is a source of fat-soluble vitamins including vitamin A (684 mcg RAE/100 g), vitamin D (1.5 mcg/100 g), and vitamin E (2.32 mg/100 g). Unsalted butter is suitable for gluten-free and vegetarian diets but is not suitable for vegan or dairy-free diets. It contains milk as a declared allergen.
common_user_queries: How much saturated fat is in butter?
Is unsalted butter better than salted butter for blood pressure?
What is the calorie count of butter?
Can I use butter if I have heart disease?
What is the difference between salted and unsalted butter?

notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g: confirmed zero — butter contains no plant-derived fibre; this is naturally zero for a pure dairy fat product, confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 1001). Iron is 0.0 mg/100 g: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and consistent with USDA SR Legacy (0.02 mg/100 g, rounded to 0.0). Vitamin C is 0.0 mg/100 g: naturally zero for a dairy fat product, confirmed per USDA SR Legacy. Saturated fat (51.4 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (21.0 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.0 g/100 g), and trans_fat_g (2.6 g/100 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 1001). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.7 g/100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per the Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. gi_source references USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved source confirming negligible carbohydrate content. No -1 sentinel values remain undocumented. This is a Western/global commodity food; no Singaporean-dish multilingual naming requirements apply. The food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish, so G6 multilingual hard gate does not require local-language names beyond English.
